metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify per-gate re-raise vs degrade semantics: specify that plan-review/code-review infra exceptions should write the ERROR sidecar then still degrade to INDETERMINATE (preserving existing contract), while the completion gate's re-raise path should write the ERROR sidecar before propagating.
evidence: Plan: 'on infra, write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
gate_dispatch.py L140-143: except LLMUnavailableError as exc: return _degraded_plan_review_verdict(...) — no re-raise
gate_dispatch.py L177-180: except LLMUnavailableError as exc: return _degraded_plan_review_verdict(...) — no re-raise
gate_dispatch.py L536-537: except LLMUnavailableError as exc: return _degraded_code_review_verdict(...) — no re-raise
gate_dispatch.py L609-610: except LLMUnavailableError as exc: return _degraded_code_review_verdict(...) — no re-raise
gate_dispatch.py L652: runner_sel.preflight() — raises LLMUnavailableError (completion gate, re-raises)
findings.finding: The plan says 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising,' but on the plan-review gate (and code-review gate), infra exceptions (LLMUnavailableError) are currently caught and converted to an INDETERMINATE verdict — they are NOT re-raised. Implementing 'write sidecar then re-raise' on those paths would break the existing fail-soft contract (replacing a graceful INDETERMINATE degrade with a hard propagated exception). Only the completion gate re-raises from preflight. The plan does not reconcile this asymmetry.
scenarios: Implementing 'write sidecar then re-raise' on plan-review replaces the INDETERMINATE degrade with an uncaught exception, breaking the close gate's soft-failure contract and potentially surfacing raw exceptions to callers instead of structured INDETERMINATE verdicts.
If instead the plan intends 'write sidecar then still degrade to INDETERMINATE' for plan-review, the 're-raise' language is wrong and misleads the implementer.
findings.suggested_fix: Split the approach into two cases: (a) gates that already degrade (plan-review, code-review): write ERROR sidecar in the catch block before calling _degraded_*_verdict — still returns INDETERMINATE, no re-raise; (b) completion gate which already re-raises: write ERROR sidecar immediately before the raise (in produce_completion_verdict's mid-run failure branch and preflight path). Update the Tests section to exercise both behaviors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the re-raise vs. soft-degrade conflict: specify, per gate (plan-review / completion / code-review), whether the ERROR sidecar is written in addition to returning INDETERMINATE (additive, no degrade-contract change) or whether the exception is now propagated (changing the plan-review degrade semantics), and update AC1 to match the actual propagation behavior of each gate.
evidence: gate_dispatch.py lines 138-143: 'except LLMUnavailableError as exc: return _degraded_plan_review_verdict(...)' — returns, does not raise
gate_dispatch.py lines 177-180: second except block also returns, does not raise
Plan: 'on infra, write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
AC1: 'injecting an infra exception yields a persisted sidecar with verdict=ERROR … and the exception still propagates'
findings.finding: The plan asserts that gate_dispatch will 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' on the infra-exception path, but the existing infra-exception handling in gate_dispatch does NOT re-raise: `produce_plan_review_verdict`'s two `except LLMUnavailableError` blocks both `return _degraded_plan_review_verdict(...)` (a soft degrade); only `produce_completion_verdict` propagates. Gating ERROR+re-raise against the current soft-degrade design is an unverified structural change claim that would require one of the two paths to be rearchitected.
scenarios: For plan-review, the exception currently does NOT propagate — the gate softly returns INDETERMINATE. To both write ERROR and re-raise, the plan must either change the plan-review degrade contract (breaking the soft-degrade-to-INDETERMINATE guarantee) or add a separate ERROR write that doesn't change the return path (which would be additive but leaves the 're-raise' claim inaccurate for plan-review)

For completion-review, the exception already propagates (produce_completion_verdict raises LLMError), so writing a sidecar before propagation is coherent — but this distinction between the two gates is absent from the plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the schema tag for the ERROR payload (e.g. `plan_review_result_v2` with `verdict='ERROR'` fields additive, or a new `_v3` tag) and specify whether `latest_review_result` / `latest_fail_verdict` readers should accept or skip it, so the sidecar is actually queryable.
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py lines 149-151: schema guard accepts only `plan_review_result_v1` and `plan_review_result_v2`
completion_sidecar.py line 125: schema guard accepts only `SCHEMA` ('completion_verifier_fail_v1')
Plan text: 'no new event type is introduced' — but does not say whether the ERROR payload reuses the existing schema tag or introduces a new schema version string
If ERROR reuses `plan_review_result_v2`, older readers would accept it but see `verdict='ERROR'` with no handling; if a new tag is used, existing readers skip it entirely
findings.finding: The new ERROR payload needs a schema tag (analogous to `plan_review_result_v2` / `completion_verifier_fail_v1`) but none is named in the plan. Existing readers in both sidecar modules have hard schema guards that skip any unrecognised schema string — meaning an ERROR payload written under an undeclared or novel schema tag will be silently ignored by `latest_review_result` / `latest_fail_verdict`.
findings.location: ## Files — `sidecar.py`, `completion_sidecar.py` — 'add an optional ERROR verdict + error object to the payload builders'
scenarios: ERROR payload written with schema 'plan_review_result_v3' → `latest_review_result` skips it → diagnosis interval is unreadable offline
ERROR payload written with schema 'plan_review_result_v2' → readers accept it but `latest_review_result` may hand it to callers expecting only PASS/BLOCK payloads, causing key-error downstream

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that the ERROR payload branch in `build_payload` must bypass (or guard before calling) `reconcile_verdict`, since `reconcile_verdict` coerces any non-PASS to FAIL and would overwrite the ERROR verdict.
evidence: completion_sidecar.py line 197: `reconcile_verdict(v)` is called unconditionally inside `build_payload`
completion.py line 215: `reconcile_verdict` normalises to exactly `PASS` or `FAIL` — `ERROR` would become `FAIL`
Plan text: 'add an optional `ERROR` verdict + `error` object to the payload builders (default-absent)' — no mention of bypassing reconcile_verdict
findings.finding: Both `completion_sidecar.build_payload` and `plan_review/sidecar.build_payload` route through `reconcile_verdict` (completion side) or assume `verdict` is already a PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE string. `reconcile_verdict` normalises any non-PASS verdict to FAIL, which would overwrite `verdict='ERROR'` before it reaches the persisted payload. The plan says 'add an optional ERROR verdict … to the payload builders' but does not specify whether the ERROR path bypasses this guardrail.
scenarios: Implementer adds ERROR branch inside build_payload after reconcile_verdict — ERROR is preserved but reconcile already mutated the copy, losing fidelity
Implementer adds ERROR branch before reconcile_verdict call — then guards it correctly, but this is not specified
findings.suggested_fix: Add an early-return branch at the top of `build_payload`: if the incoming verdict dict already carries `verdict='ERROR'` (or the builder is called via a dedicated `build_error_payload` helper), emit the ERROR schema directly without calling `reconcile_verdict`.

findings.finding: The schema-guarded readers in plan_review/sidecar.py (latest_review_result, all_review_results) only accept schema values 'plan_review_result_v1'/'plan_review_result_v2'; an ERROR record written to REVIEW_RESULT with a distinct or unspecified schema will be silently skipped by these readers. The plan does not specify the schema tag for the ERROR payload, nor enumerate these readers as consumers that may need updating.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` — add optional ERROR verdict + error object to payload builders.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) introduce a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_error_v1`) and confirm the existing schema guards correctly skip it (readers see no ERROR rows — intentional), or (b) add that tag to the guard list and define what the ERROR payload looks like when returned. Document the choice explicitly in the Approach section.

findings.finding: The plan adds the ERROR outcome to completion_sidecar.py's payload builder, but does not enumerate latest_fail_verdict() and latest_pass_record() — both of which are schema-guarded to 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'/'completion_verifier_pass_v1' — as consumers to evaluate. An ERROR record under COMPLETION_VERDICT with a different or unspecified schema will be silently skipped by both readers.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` — add optional ERROR verdict + error object to payload builders.

findings.finding: The plan's core mechanism — 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' — does not match the actual plan-review and code-review gate control flow, where infra exceptions are caught and RETURNED as INDETERMINATE verdicts, not re-raised. Only the completion gate propagates the exception. The implementer following the plan's 'before re-raising' framing will look for a try/except…raise site that does not exist on the plan-review path, leading to either a misplaced write or a missed write.
findings.location: ## Approach / gate_dispatch.py lines 138-143, 177-180 (plan-review catches LLMUnavailableError and returns INDETERMINATE, never re-raises); lines 652-682 (completion propagates)
findings.suggested_fix: For plan-review: write the ERROR sidecar at the top of `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` (before partitioning/returning), passing `ctx.ticket_id` as the ticket anchor. For code-review: write inside `_degraded_code_review_verdict` when `request.target_ticket` is non-None (no ticket, no sidecar). For completion: write before the `raise LLMError(...)` at line 679, using `ticket_id`. The plan should describe these three distinct insertion points rather than a single 'before re-raising' framing.

findings.finding: The schema tag for the ERROR sidecar record is unspecified. Both sidecar modules apply schema-string guards on reads (`'plan_review_result_v2'` in `latest_review_result`; `SCHEMA`/`SCHEMA_PASS` in `latest_fail_verdict`/`latest_pass_record`). If the ERROR record reuses an existing schema tag, existing readers will return it unexpectedly (callers never expect `verdict='ERROR'`); if it uses a new schema tag, existing readers will silently skip it — neither outcome is specified and no reader update is mentioned.
findings.location: Files section / sidecar.py, completion_sidecar.py; Approach section / 'no new event type is introduced'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_result_error_v1`) and update the schema guards in both readers to explicitly skip it, so they continue returning only PASS/BLOCK/FAIL records while the ERROR record is separately queryable by a new reader or direct jq.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile `error.evidence_ref`: either add an AC/test assertion for it, or explicitly mark it as optional/nullable in the What section so implementers and reviewers know its presence is not required.
evidence: What: 'an error{cause,evidence_ref} object on the gates' infrastructure-exception path'
AC1: 'non-empty error.cause' — no mention of evidence_ref
Tests section: 'non-empty error.cause' — same omission
findings.finding: The `What` section describes the `error` object as having two fields — `cause` and `evidence_ref` — but neither AC nor the Tests section verifies `evidence_ref`, leaving it as an uncovered deliverable component with no acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What section (`error{cause,evidence_ref}`) vs Acceptance Criteria (only checks `error.cause`)
scenarios: Implementer may omit evidence_ref entirely since it is never tested, making the What description misleading or the implementation incomplete.
Alternatively, if evidence_ref is optional/nullable by design, the plan should say so explicitly to avoid ambiguity.
findings.suggested_fix: If evidence_ref is optional, update the What section to: `error{cause, evidence_ref?}` and add a note that it is populated when a relevant log ref exists. If it is required, add to AC1: 'and `.error.evidence_ref` is a string (may be empty)' and update the unit test accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `latest_review_result`, `all_review_results`, `attest.py`, and `plan_review/__init__.py` as consumers that may receive an ERROR sidecar as their 'latest' payload, and specify how they should detect/skip ERROR records (e.g. by a distinct schema tag, a schema guard on `verdict`, or a separate event type for ERROR records).
evidence: Plan: 'The REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars are non-replay events … the natural home, so no new event type is introduced.'
sidecar.py latest_review_result: schema guard accepts 'plan_review_result_v1' and 'plan_review_result_v2' — if ERROR record uses same schema, it is returned as the latest payload.
plan_review/__init__.py:193 calls sidecar.latest_review_result for novelty prior-findings; attest.py:279,320 calls it for remediation-eligibility.
Plan is silent on how these callers should behave when the latest sidecar has verdict='ERROR'.
findings.finding: Readers of the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar — specifically `latest_review_result`, `all_review_results`, `attest.py` (remediation-mode eligibility), and `plan_review/__init__.py` (novelty prior-findings) — are not enumerated as consumers that the plan's ERROR sidecar write could affect. If an ERROR record is emitted as a `REVIEW_RESULT` event with the same schema tag (`plan_review_result_v2`), `latest_review_result` will return it as the newest valid payload, injecting `verdict='ERROR'` into the remediation-eligibility check (`attest.py`) and the novelty prior-findings set (`plan_review/__init__.py`). The plan does not address how these readers should handle or ignore ERROR payloads.
scenarios: An infra exception occurs during a plan-review gate run; the ERROR sidecar is written as REVIEW_RESULT with schema plan_review_result_v2 and verdict='ERROR'; the next re-review call reads it via latest_review_result and the remediation-eligibility check in attest.py evaluates a record with no findings and verdict='ERROR', possibly incorrectly granting or denying remediation mode.
The novelty prior-findings set for Pass-2 is populated from an ERROR record (findings=[]), making the sub-call believe there are no prior findings and suppressing recall.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_error_v1`) for ERROR payloads so the schema guards in `latest_review_result` and `latest_fail_verdict` skip them by construction — no reader changes required, and the ERROR record is still persisted and compaction-preserved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `evidence_ref` in the plan: its type (string? URI?), what it should contain (e.g., last N lines of provider response, a log correlation ID, or `null` if unavailable), and whether it is optional.
evidence: 'an `error{cause,evidence_ref}` object' — `evidence_ref` appears once with no further definition
The Tests and ACs sections only assert on `error.cause`, leaving `evidence_ref` unverified and unspecified
findings.finding: The `error` object schema includes an `evidence_ref` field that is never defined — its type, what it references (a log line, a span ID, a file path?), and whether it is optional or required are all unstated. An executor cannot implement this field without guessing.
scenarios: Executor leaves evidence_ref as null/empty, defeating its diagnostic purpose.
Executor populates it with a local file path that means nothing in a CI log context.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '`evidence_ref`: optional string, the raw provider error message or HTTP response body excerpt (≤500 chars), or null if unavailable. Omit the key rather than write null to keep the payload clean.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `error.cause` should reuse `failure.outcome_of()` + `failure.resolution_fields()` (already sanitized, classified) rather than introducing a parallel struct; if a separate `error` object is still needed, explain why the existing `coverage.llm_error` + `resolution_fields` machinery is insufficient.
evidence: failure.py:342 `resolution_fields(outcome)` → `{resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` already the single writer of disposition shape
gate_dispatch.py:417 `'llm_error': str(error)` already written to coverage on degraded path
gate_dispatch.py:418 `**_failure.resolution_fields(outcome)` already spreads classified cause onto coverage

Plan: 'error{cause,evidence_ref} object' — `cause` overlaps with `diagnostic.message`/`llm_error`; no explanation for why a parallel struct is preferred over reusing `outcome_of()` + `resolution_fields()`
findings.finding: The proposed `error.cause` sub-object on the ERROR sidecar payload partially rebuilds existing classified-failure machinery: `failure.py` already exposes `outcome_of(error)` → `resolution_fields(outcome)` producing `{resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` (sanitized, allowlisted), and the degraded-verdict paths already write `coverage.llm_error = str(error)` and `**resolution_fields(outcome)`. The plan does not explain why a parallel `error{cause, ...}` struct is needed rather than reusing this existing pipeline.
scenarios: Two parallel representations of the same infra failure cause exist in the store (coverage.llm_error + error.cause), diverging over time as one gets richer sanitization than the other.
The new `error.cause` bypasses the allowlist/redactor in `sanitize_diagnostic`, risking leaking raw provider messages (e.g. API keys in error strings) into the sidecar.
findings.suggested_fix: In `build_payload` for the ERROR branch, derive `error.cause` from `failure.outcome_of(exc).diagnostic` (already allowlisted + redacted) rather than from `str(exc)` directly, aligning with the existing sanitized-diagnostic pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the type and referent of `error.evidence_ref` (e.g. `str | None` = sanitized LLMOutcome diagnostic key, or a sidecar event UUID, or `null` when not available) so payload builders and tests can emit and assert a concrete value.
evidence: Plan text: 'an additive `verdict="ERROR"` outcome with an `error{cause,evidence_ref}` object'
No definition of `evidence_ref` in plan_review/sidecar.py, completion_sidecar.py, failure.py, or errors.py (confirmed by search)
The AC only asserts `error.cause` is non-empty — `evidence_ref` is mentioned in the schema shape but not tested or defined
findings.finding: The `error` object's `evidence_ref` field is named in the plan but never defined — its type, format, and referent (e.g. a log-file path, an LLMOutcome diagnostic key, a sidecar event UUID) are unspecified. An implementer cannot write or test the payload builder without knowing what this field holds.
scenarios: Implementer sets evidence_ref to a file path; consumer expects an event UUID → silent mismatch
Test author omits evidence_ref entirely; schema-guard downstream breaks for a consumer that expects it

findings.finding: `error.evidence_ref` is named but never defined: the plan does not specify what artifact it references (a sidecar event id, a log URI, the `diagnostic` dict from `LLMOutcome`, a coverage field, or something else), making the field semantically unimplementable.

The `error` object schema includes `evidence_ref` (named in the What section) but its content, format, and whether it is required or optional are never defined, and the ACs do not validate it — leaving the executor to invent a schema.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add 'evidence_ref: nullable string — the row_id of the most recent REVIEW_RESULT or COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar at the moment of failure; null if unavailable' and add `jq -e '.error.evidence_ref != null'` (or `.error.evidence_ref | type == "string"`) to AC1; or (b) drop evidence_ref from the spec until it is designed.

findings.finding: The `error.cause` field's value domain is never defined: it is unclear whether `cause` is a free-form string (e.g. `str(exc)`), a `ResolutionClass` enum value (the 8-class vocabulary already in `failure.py`), or a structured dict. An implementer cannot determine the type/vocabulary from the plan alone.
findings.location: Approach section / 'error{cause,evidence_ref} object'; Files: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py, src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: `cause` = `str(exc)` (human-readable, always non-empty); optionally add `resolution_class` as a parallel field carrying the `ResolutionClass.value` string when `failure.outcome_of(exc)` is non-None.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a purpose-built ERROR payload shape (a slim dict with schema, ticket_id, gate, error.cause, error.evidence_ref, timestamp) written directly in gate_dispatch.py or a new helper — rather than routing through the existing verdict-oriented build_payload functions — to avoid the structural mismatch.
evidence: Plan: 'add an optional ERROR verdict + error object to the payload builders (default-absent)'
completion_sidecar.py L182-218: build_payload receives a verdict dict, calls reconcile_verdict(v), branches on verdict string PASS vs FAIL, accesses v.get('findings'), v.get('remediation') etc.

plan_review/sidecar.py L394-516: build_payload accesses verdict.get('blocking'), verdict.get('advisory'), verdict.get('dropped'), iterates all_findings, stamps verified_at_sha — all verdict-dependent fields absent on an infra abort
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding an optional `verdict="ERROR"` and `error` field to existing sidecar payload builders (build_payload in plan_review/sidecar.py and completion_sidecar.py), but these builders receive a completed verdict dict (PASS/BLOCK/FAIL/INDETERMINATE). At the point of an infra exception, no verdict dict exists yet — there is no blocking/findings/coaching data to pass to build_payload. Grafting an error field onto these builders creates a structural mismatch: the builder's existing logic (reconcile_verdict, _slim(f) for all_findings, etc.) assumes a real verdict payload.
scenarios: Calling plan_review/sidecar.build_payload with a synthetic {'verdict': 'ERROR', 'error': {...}} dict silently produces a payload with empty findings/coverage/coaching/metrics because the expected keys are absent — the sidecar exists but carries no useful diagnosis context.
Alternatively adding an error fast-path branch inside build_payload adds complexity without cleanliness — it effectively makes build_payload handle two incompatible input shapes.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the code-review ERROR sidecar behavior when `target_ticket` is None (e.g., skip the sidecar emit silently — consistent with the existing 'sidecar only if target_ticket' note in produce_code_review_verdict's docstring).
evidence: gate_dispatch.py L495: session_id: str | None = None
gate_dispatch.py L499: target_ticket: str | None = None — explicitly optional
Plan scope: 'Only gate-encountered infra failures are captured' — does not distinguish ticketed vs. ticketless code-review runs
findings.finding: The code-review gate's `target_ticket` is optional (`None` for local `rebar review-code` runs that don't name a ticket). The plan does not specify what happens when `target_ticket` is None and an infra exception occurs — there is no ticket to write the ERROR sidecar to.
scenarios: A local rebar review-code run with no --ticket flag hits a 5xx — target_ticket is None, the ERROR sidecar emit has no ticket_id to write to, causing either a crash in append_event or a silently swallowed no-op, neither of which is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a guard: emit the ERROR sidecar only when a ticket_id is available (target_ticket is not None for code-review, ctx.ticket_id for plan-review, ticket_id param for completion). The docstring for produce_code_review_verdict already says 'sidecar only if target_ticket' — mirror that pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either drop `evidence_ref` from the `error` object (it has no defined consumer or AC coverage) or add an AC that specifies its value and asserts it in the unit test.
evidence: Search for 'evidence_ref' across entire repo: zero hits
AC only asserts: 'non-empty error.cause' — evidence_ref is neither tested nor consumed
Plan does not define what evidence_ref references or which consumer reads it
findings.finding: The `evidence_ref` field inside the proposed `error{cause, evidence_ref}` object has zero existing call-sites or usages anywhere in the codebase (no matches for `evidence_ref`), and no AC item requires it to be non-null or asserted in the unit tests — the AC only asserts `non-empty error.cause`. This is a YAGNI field: it adds schema surface for a hypothetical future consumer without a current done-definition.
scenarios: The field ships as always-null/always-absent and accumulates dead schema weight.
A future consumer assumes a specific format for evidence_ref that was never pinned, causing a silent incompatibility.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit assertion in the Approach/Rollback section about the persisted store state after rollback: confirm that pre-existing ERROR sidecar rows are either (a) harmless to all post-rollback readers (cite why), or (b) require a compensating forward step (e.g., a one-time rewrite of orphaned ERROR rows to a tombstone/ignored state).
evidence: "back-out = stop writing ERROR"
"non-replay events that survive compaction"
ABSENCE: no assertion that the store state is valid/safe after rollback with pre-existing ERROR rows
POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE sub-criterion: plan must assert schema state after rollback, not merely the action taken
findings.finding: The rollback description ('back-out = stop writing ERROR') is a behavioral action only and does not assert the persisted store state after a partial rollback. ERROR sidecar records already written before rollback remain in the store permanently. The plan does not assert whether those orphaned ERROR rows (diagnosis intervals that opened but will never close) are safe for readers, downstream analytics, or the compaction logic that 'already survive compaction'.
scenarios: Feature is deployed, some ERROR sidecars are written, then the feature is rolled back by stopping writes; orphaned ERROR sidecars remain and may cause open diagnosis intervals that never close, confusing downstream consumers or analytics.

A reader upgraded past rollback encounters unexpected `verdict='ERROR'` rows it was not designed to handle if the rollback assumption ('older readers see no ERROR rows') does not hold for partially-upgraded deployments.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'After rollback, any already-persisted ERROR sidecars remain in the store but are inert: (1) pre-rollback readers already ignore unknown verdict values per the back-compat guarantee; (2) diagnosis-interval logic treats an ERROR row with no subsequent PASS as an open interval and must already handle open intervals gracefully (cite existing handling or add a guard). No data is lost and no compensating write is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and update the Approach premise: `completion_sidecar.py`'s `build_payload` has no `metrics` key (confirmed by reading lines 182–219); clarify where `verdict="ERROR"` and the `error{cause,evidence_ref}` object should be placed in the COMPLETION_VERDICT payload, since the 'already alongside metrics' rationale does not apply to it.
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py:497: '"metrics": (verdict.get("coverage", {}) or {}).get("metrics", {})' — metrics field IS present
completion_sidecar.py:182-219: build_payload returns a dict with schema, verdict, ticket_id, findings, remediation, certifiable, runner, model, material_fingerprint — NO 'metrics' key anywhere
Plan quote: 'The REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars … already carry a metrics block — the natural home, so no new event type is introduced'
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars … already carry a metrics block' as justification for these being 'the natural home' of the new ERROR verdict. This is only true for REVIEW_RESULT (plan_review/sidecar.py:497 confirms a top-level 'metrics' key in build_payload). Reading completion_sidecar.py:182-219 shows that build_payload for COMPLETION_VERDICT carries no 'metrics' field at all. The shared rationale rests on a partially false premise for one of the two named files.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars are non-replay events that survive compaction and already carry a metrics block — the natural home'; Files section — 'src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py'
scenarios: An implementer reads the plan and assumes both sidecar builders already have a metrics block to extend alongside the new error field; they may be surprised by the structural asymmetry and incorrectly add metrics to COMPLETION_VERDICT when it was never there, or misplace the ERROR object.
Reviewers may not probe completion_sidecar.py and accept the rationale as uniformly applying to both sidecars, missing that only one of the two named files actually has the asserted property.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the Approach to note that REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT have different existing shapes (REVIEW_RESULT has a top-level 'metrics' key; COMPLETION_VERDICT does not), and explicitly specify the target field location in build_payload for each sidecar independently (e.g., top-level 'verdict' and 'error' keys in COMPLETION_VERDICT's returned dict).

findings.finding: The plan asserts both `REVIEW_RESULT`/`COMPLETION_VERDICT` sidecars 'already carry a `metrics` block' and calls them 'the natural home' for the new `error{}` object — but `completion_sidecar.py`'s `build_payload` contains no `metrics` field in either the SCHEMA (FAIL) or SCHEMA_PASS payloads. The assertion is only true of `plan_review/sidecar.py`. The plan's premise that the completion sidecar is a symmetric peer carrying metrics is unverified.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars are non-replay events that survive compaction and already carry a metrics block — the natural home'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'already carry a metrics block — the natural home' with separate statements: plan_review sidecar carries `metrics` (verified); completion sidecar has no `metrics` block and the ERROR payload placement must be specified explicitly for each file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical exception-type list (e.g., `ConnectionError`, `httpx.HTTPStatusError` with 5xx, `TimeoutError`) that map to infra vs domain in the plan or in an inline comment contract in gate_dispatch.py.
evidence: 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL' — no exception types or HTTP codes enumerated
The unit test plan only says 'raising a connection error' — one example, not the full classification contract
findings.finding: The plan specifies that gate_dispatch.py should 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL' but never enumerates which Python exception types or HTTP status codes map to each category. An executor must invent this classification without guidance, risking inconsistent behavior (e.g., is a 429 RateLimitError infra or domain? Is a JSONDecodeError infra or domain?).
scenarios: A 429 Too Many Requests could be classified as infra (rate limit = provider issue) or domain (gate policy) depending on interpretation.
A JSONDecodeError from a malformed provider response might be misclassified, causing missed or spurious ERROR sidecars.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet: 'Infra exceptions: `ConnectionError`, `TimeoutError`, provider HTTP 5xx (`httpx.HTTPStatusError` where `response.status_code >= 500`). Domain exceptions: anything the gate framework raises as a BLOCK/FAIL result object. All others: re-raise without writing ERROR sidecar.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cardinality assertion to AC1: assert exactly one ERROR sidecar is written per infra exception event (e.g., `jq 'length == 1'` on the collection of ERROR-verdict records for that gate run).
evidence: 'injecting an infra exception yields a persisted sidecar with `jq -e \'.verdict=="ERROR"\'' — no assertion on count
No mention of retry/re-raise loop guard in gate_dispatch.py
findings.finding: AC1 asserts that a sidecar with `verdict=="ERROR"` is written but includes no cardinality assertion. If the gate code or test fixture triggers multiple sidecar writes for a single exception (e.g., a retry loop), the AC passes even though the behavior would be incorrect.
scenarios: A retry wrapper in gate_dispatch.py catches and re-raises the infra exception twice, writing two ERROR sidecars for one logical failure event.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC1: 'a stubbed gate run raising an infra exception writes EXACTLY ONE sidecar with `verdict=="ERROR"` (assert `jq -e \'[.[] | select(.verdict=="ERROR")] | length == 1\'` on the output file or mock call list) and non-empty `error.cause`, then re-raises.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the sidecar write on the error path: wrap the write in try/except so that a write failure logs a warning and falls through, preserving the original infra exception for re-raise.
evidence: Plan states 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' with no mention of handling a write failure
If sidecar write raises, the original infra exception may be replaced or suppressed in the call stack
findings.finding: The sidecar write on the error path has no specified error handling: if the write itself throws (disk full, permissions, etc.), the plan does not say whether the original exception is preserved or masked, nor whether a silent no-write is acceptable.
findings.location: Files: src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py — 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
scenarios: Disk full or permission-denied during sidecar write on infra exception path
Sidecar write raises an unrelated exception, masking the original provider 5xx error from callers
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the sidecar write in a bare `except Exception: log.warning(...)` so write failures are surfaced as a log warning but never replace the original exception; the original infra exception is then re-raised unconditionally.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either make the infra-exception classification set configurable/extensible (e.g., a registered exception-type list) or add an ADR/AGENTS.md entry describing the ERROR-verdict diagnostic pattern and how to extend the infra-exception classifier.
evidence: Plan asserts 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling)' without specifying configurability
Plan introduces a new diagnostic pattern (ERROR-then-PASS intervals, error{cause,evidence_ref} contract) that future gate authors must follow, with no doc update specified
findings.finding: The infra-exception classification criteria ('provider 5xx/connection/tooling') constitute an evolving business rule hardcoded in gate_dispatch.py with no configurable allowlist or documented extension point, and no ADR/AGENTS.md update is planned for this new ERROR-verdict diagnostic pattern.
findings.location: Files section: src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py — 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL'; Approach section: no ADR/doc update mentioned
scenarios: A new LLM provider added later raises a different exception type not covered by the hardcoded classification — silently misclassified as domain BLOCK
A future gate author doesn't know about the ERROR-verdict contract and omits it, creating inconsistent diagnostics

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retirement/cleanup path for ERROR sidecar records (e.g., TTL, archival after interval closes with a PASS, or explicit pruning policy) so they do not accumulate indefinitely.
evidence: "non-replay events that survive compaction" — plan explicitly confirms ERROR records are not pruned by compaction
No RETIRE/cleanup path appears anywhere in the plan, Files list, or Acceptance Criteria
findings.finding: The ERROR sidecar records accumulate indefinitely with no defined retirement or cleanup path. The plan explicitly states these records 'survive compaction', meaning the compaction mechanism does not prune them, and no other pruning/expiry/archival path is described. Without a retirement path, ERROR records for ephemeral or repeated gate runs will grow unboundedly in the sidecar store.
findings.location: Approach section: 'non-replay events that survive compaction'
scenarios: A long-running project with repeated infra failures accumulates ERROR sidecars forever with no cleanup, causing unbounded storage growth and degraded query performance on the sidecar store.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the consumer(s) of ERROR verdict records and specify their behavior for edge cases: absent PASS close, duplicate ERROR records, and stale open intervals.
evidence: "a diagnosis interval starts (ERROR) and ends (next PASS) is recorded" — implies a pairing consumer
"older readers see no ERROR rows" — implies newer readers exist but they are never named or specified
No consumer is listed in Files or Acceptance Criteria
findings.finding: The consumers of ERROR verdict records are not enumerated. The plan describes ERROR as opening a 'diagnosis interval' that closes on the next PASS, implying a downstream reader must pair ERROR and PASS records to compute interval metrics — but no such consumer is named, and no contract is stated for how consumers should handle absent, duplicate, or unmatched ERROR records (e.g., an ERROR with no subsequent PASS).
findings.location: What section: 'a diagnosis interval starts (ERROR) and ends (next PASS) is recorded by gate code'
scenarios: A downstream analytics or reporting consumer encounters an ERROR record with no subsequent PASS (e.g., the gate is abandoned) and has no contract for whether to treat the interval as open, discard it, or surface an alert — leading to silent data quality issues.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an idempotency strategy for ERROR sidecar writes (e.g., a gate-run-scoped deduplication key, at-most-once write guard, or explicit tolerance of duplicates in the consumer contract) to prevent phantom intervals on retry.
evidence: Plan states the exception is re-raised: 'on infra, write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
AC asserts 'the exception still propagates' — caller receives the exception and may retry
No idempotency key, dedup check, or at-most-once guard is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not address idempotency of ERROR sidecar writes under retry or at-least-once delivery. If a gate is retried after an infra exception (a common pattern — the exception is re-raised and the caller may retry), gate code will write a second ERROR sidecar for the same logical failure, producing duplicate open interval markers with no deduplication key or merge strategy.
findings.location: Approach section and AC: 'injecting an infra exception yields a persisted sidecar ... and the exception still propagates'
scenarios: A caller retries a gate after an infra connection error; each attempt writes a fresh ERROR sidecar, producing N duplicate open-interval records for a single logical failure. A downstream consumer pairing ERRORs to the closing PASS now sees N-1 phantom unclosed intervals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prior-art comparison in ## Approach: show which specific failure paths (plan-review preflight, completion gate propagation, code-review mid-run) do NOT currently write a durable infra-failure sidecar, making verdict=ERROR additive rather than redundant vs the existing `coverage.llm_unavailable` + `resolution_class` data already written by `_degraded_*_verdict()` helpers.
evidence: Plan: 'so a diagnosis interval starts (ERROR) and ends (next PASS) is recorded by gate code, never by agent self-report'

gate_dispatch.py lines 395-426: _degraded_plan_review_verdict() writes a sidecar with coverage.llm_unavailable=True + resolution_fields() (resolution_class/retryable/diagnostic) on every infra failure, already gate-code-stamped
failure.py lines 342-354: resolution_fields() is the single writer of the disposition shape; it already persists the infra failure classification durably
ABSENCE: plan contains no comparison with existing INDETERMINATE-sidecar interval query to show the gap
findings.finding: The plan's core motivation — recording a durable, gate-stamped 'diagnosis interval start (ERROR)' so the interval end (next PASS) is recoverable — is already addressed by the existing INDETERMINATE sidecar machinery: `gate_dispatch._degraded_plan_review_verdict()` / `_degraded_code_review_verdict()` already write a REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar with `coverage.llm_unavailable=True`, `coverage.resolution_class`, `coverage.retryable`, `coverage.diagnostic`, and a timestamp-prefixed filename — giving an offline consumer the interval start, the error classification, and (via the next verdict=PASS sidecar) the interval end. The plan does not demonstrate what the existing INDETERMINATE-→-PASS transition query lacks that verdict=ERROR + `error{cause,evidence_ref}` would supply.
scenarios: An offline consumer querying REVIEW_RESULT sidecars for llm_unavailable=True already identifies the diagnosis start; querying for the next verdict=PASS identifies the end — the same interval the plan proposes to record with ERROR/PASS
A reviewer evaluating the plan cannot determine whether the new verdict=ERROR is additive signal or redundant repackaging of coverage.llm_unavailable + coverage.resolution_class
findings.suggested_fix: Audit each gate path in gate_dispatch.py: produce_plan_review_verdict (except LLMUnavailableError → _degraded_plan_review_verdict → sidecar written ✓), produce_completion_verdict (raises LLMError, no sidecar → gap ✓), produce_code_review_verdict (_degraded_code_review_verdict → sidecar only if target_ticket, miss on local runs). Document these specific gaps as the motivation for verdict=ERROR; the plan becomes concrete prior-art-aware justification rather than reinventing what already exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the classification rule co-located with the ERROR payload shape: which exception types (e.g. `LLMUnavailableError` and subclasses, or `ResolutionClass` values in `{WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL}`) count as 'infra' and trigger the ERROR sidecar, vs which are 'domain' and do not.
evidence: Plan text: 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL; on infra, write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
Existing hierarchy in errors.py: LLMError → LLMUnavailableError / LLMRunnerError / StructuredOutputError — none labelled 'infra' vs 'domain'

ResolutionClass enum has 8 values (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) — plan does not say which map to 'infra'
The AC unit test uses 'a connection error' as the infra example, but no rule for the general case is given
findings.finding: The classification boundary between 'infra exception' (→ write ERROR sidecar) and 'domain BLOCK/FAIL' (→ no ERROR sidecar) is stated as a goal for `gate_dispatch.py` but the decision rule — which exception types or exception attributes qualify as 'infra' — is never specified. The existing codebase has `LLMUnavailableError`, `LLMError`, `LLMRunnerError`, and `ResolutionClass` (8 values), none of which are explicitly mapped to the infra/domain split in the plan.
findings.location: ## Files — `gate_dispatch.py` — 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL'
scenarios: A StructuredOutputError (model produced bad output) — is that 'infra' or 'domain'? Plan is silent.
A CHANGE_INPUT resolution class (input too large) — infra or domain? Plan does not say.
Two implementers pick different exception types → inconsistent ERROR sidecar emission
findings.suggested_fix: Map 'infra' to `LLMUnavailableError` (and subclasses) plus any exception whose `ResolutionClass` is in `{WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL}` — these represent provider-side failures, not agent/input defects. Treat `StructuredOutputError`, `LLMRunnerError` without a provider disposition, and domain `BLOCK/FAIL` verdicts as non-infra.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
